title: Regex Strings for Pattern Matching
text: Regex Strings for Pattern Matching Uniface enables you to determine if the data in a string value matches a desired pattern using regex strings. A regex string is a regular expression enclosed in forward slashes (‘ / ’). Regex strings can be used to specify a field syntax, such as postal code or telephone number. They are also useful when searching for and comparing or replacing strings. For example, they can be used when: Defining logical expressions with the = , == , and != operators in conditional ProcScript statements ( if , repeat , selectcase , or while ). Searching and replacing strings using scan , $scan , $rscan , $replace . Truncating strings using $ltrim and $rtrim . Splitting a string using $split . Explicitly converting data to a specified data type using $typed . Example: Using Regex Strings To represent the pattern of a Canadian postal code (such as B2Y 5K2), the regex string would be /[A-Z]\d[A-Z] \d[A-Z]\d/ . To enforce this pattern in a data entry field, you can specify this regex string in the Field Syntax property. In ProcScript, you can use $regex to convert a string to a regex string, and use this to search for a matching string. For example: Explain this code ;Field ADDRESS = "Mary John, 2 Ginger Lane, Genoa NS B0R 1N6" ; Scan the string for a substring that matches the postal code pattern, ; and return the position of the matching string vLocation = $scan (ADDRESS, $regex("[A-Z]\d[A-Z] \d[A-Z]\d")) ; If a position is returned, issue a message if (vLocation > 0) putmess "A postal code is provided" endif Copy Syntax of Regex Strings Regex strings (regular expressions) provide a powerful way to specify patterns for text matching and manipulation. The syntax of regex strings used in Uniface conforms to a Modified ECMAScript regular expression grammar, which is a widely accepted standard for defining regex patterns. Regex strings (regular expressions) are a versatile tool for defining complex patterns in text. While they are powerful, they can also be complex. For detailed guidance on constructing regex patterns and utilizing advanced features, refer to the ECMAScript regular expression syntax documentation. Table 1.
